1: חֲבַצֶּלֶת הַשָּׁרוֹן (chavatzelet hasharon) — the specific identification of this flower is debated: the LXX translates as 'lily' (anthos), but other candidates include narcissus, meadow saffron, asphodel, or crocus. The Sharon (the coastal plain of Israel) was known for its lush vegetation; the valley lilies (shoshanat ha'amakim) appear in contrast to the cultivated highland gardens. The woman describes herself modestly — a common wildflower — before the beloved's response elevates her above all other flowers (v2).
2: כְּשׁוֹשַׁנָּה בֵּין הַחוֹחִים (keshoshanah bein hachochim) — 'like a lily among thorns' — the beloved has called herself a common wildflower; the lover responds with a pointed comparison that transforms her self-deprecation: she is not merely a flower but a lily among thorns — uniquely beautiful precisely by contrast with what surrounds her. הַחוֹחִים (hachochim) — 'thorns' uses the same root as the thorns of judgment in the prophets and Gen 3:18. The comparison elevates what she minimized.
3: כְּתַפּוּחַ בֵּין עֲצֵי הַיַּעַר (ke-tapuach bein atzei haya'ar) — 'like an apple tree among trees of the forest.' The identification of tapuach as apple is debated; the apricot and quince are also proposed (apples were not common in ancient Israel). What matters lexically is the contrast: a fruit-bearing, shade-providing, fragrant tree (taapuach is associated with fragrance, 7:9) among the non-productive forest trees — exactly mirroring the beloved's 'lily among thorns.' בְּצִלּוֹ (betzillo) — 'in his shade' — the shade of the tree as the beloved's dwelling; the protective shade is a covenant metaphor (Ps 17:8; 91:1: under the shadow of his wings).
4: בֵּית הַיַּיִן (beit hayayin) — 'house of wine' — the MKT renders 'banquet hall' which captures the social function: not simply a wine cellar but the room for celebratory drinking. דִּגְלוֹ עָלַי אַהֲבָה (diglo alay ahavah) — 'his banner over me was love' — degel (banner, standard) is a military term (Num 1:52; 2:2 — each tribe has its standard). The banner of love flying over the beloved claims her: she is under his flag, identified with him. The military image in an intimate context speaks of total claiming and total protection.
5: סַמְּכוּנִי (sammekuni) and רַפְּדוּנִי (rappeduni) — 'support me, refresh me' — both plural imperatives addressed to the daughters of Jerusalem. The beloved is overwhelmed by love — חוֹלַת אַהֲבָה (cholat ahavah) literally 'sick with love' — and she calls for physical support. The raisins (ashishot) and apples (tapuchim) as restoratives reflect the ancient use of dried fruit and cooling foods for recovery. The beloved's love-sickness is a motif in Egyptian love poetry; here it frames the complete absorption of the beloved in the relationship.
6: שְׂמֹאלוֹ (smolo) — his left arm; יְמִינוֹ (yemino) — his right arm (hand). The left-right embrace positions the beloved completely within the beloved's encompassing arms — surrounded, not merely touched. The right hand/arm in Hebrew idiom is the arm of strength and protection (Ps 89:13; Isa 41:10: I will uphold you with my righteous right hand). The full-body embrace in the Song is both intimate and protective: the beloved is covered by her beloved's strength.
7: מַשְׁבִּיעָה אֶתְכֶם (mashbia'ah etkhem) — 'I adjure you' — the verb shava' (to swear/adjure) is the same root as oath and the divine name Shaddai. The adjuration formula 'by the gazelles and the does of the field' (bitzevaot o bé'aylot hassadeh) is a wordplay: tzeviot (gazelles/does) sounds like Tzeviot (hosts, as in LORD of hosts, YHWH Tzeviot). The beloved adjures the daughters of Jerusalem by these creatures — either avoiding the divine name directly or invoking it through the euphemistic sound-alike. תָּעִירוּ (ta'iru) — 'rouse, stir up' — the love (ahavah) must come in its appointed time, not forced or hurried.
8: קוֹל דּוֹדִי (kol dodi) — 'the voice/sound of my beloved' — qol (sound, voice) is the first word of the verse; it announces before describing. מְדַלֵּג (medalleg) — 'leaping' — a Piel participle indicating vigorous, energetic movement; מְקַפֵּץ (meqapetz) — 'bounding' — a rarer verb also indicating quick movement. The beloved approaches with athletic energy, not sedately; the mountains and hills cannot slow him. The imagery anticipates theophany language (the LORD who 'comes leaping' appears in prophetic poetry).
9: מֵצִיץ מִן הַחַרַכִּים (meitzitz min hacharakim) — 'peering through the lattice windows' — the verb tzitz (to look through, to peer) shares a root with the priestly headpiece (ציץ, tzitz, the golden flower of the High Priest's mitre, Exod 28:36) and with the blossoming flower. The lover who peeps through the lattice is playfully active; the enclosed woman glimpses the one who seeks her through the screen that separates their spaces. The lattice windows of ancient Near Eastern homes provided both privacy and visibility.
10: עָנָה דוֹדִי (anah dodi) — 'my beloved spoke/responded' — the verb anah (answer, respond) implies that his speech is a response to an unspoken or implicit question. קוּמִי (qumi) — 'arise' — feminine imperative; לְכִי לָךְ (lekhi lakh) — 'come away for yourself' — the lakh (reflexive dative, for yourself) is idiomatic; it marks the invitation as for her own benefit and wholeness. The double command (arise, come away) parallels the Abrahamic call (Gen 12:1: go for yourself, lekh lekha) and the eschatological invitation.
11: הַסְּתָו עָבַר (hastav avar) — 'the winter is past' — stav (winter) appears only here in the Hebrew Bible as a season name; it may be a loanword. The perfect tense (avar = has passed) announces completed fact, not prediction. The rains (geshem) — the winter rains of Israel's agricultural calendar that came from October-March — have ended. The announcement has the character of a proclamation: the season of dormancy is definitively over. The covenant agricultural calendar is the background; the rains and dry season structured Israel's year.
12: הַנִּצָּנִים (hanietzanim) — 'the flowers/blossoms' — from the root netz, related to blossoming and sparkling (the same root gives the hawk its name for its sparkling flight). עֵת הַזָּמִיר (et hazamir) — 'the season of singing' — but zamir can mean both 'singing' and 'pruning' (of vines). The intentional ambiguity in the text allows both meanings: the season of vine-pruning and the season of song. The turtledove's voice (qol hator) — tor echoes Torah — marks the season as appointed and purposeful.
13: תְּאֵנָה חָנְטָה פַגֶּיהָ (te'enah chanetah faggeyha) — 'the fig tree is ripening its early figs' — the paggim (unripe early figs, lit. 'green figs') are the first fruit of the fig tree before the summer crop; their appearance marks late spring. The word chanetah (has spiced, has conditioned, has prepared) is unusual — normally the fig 'forms' its fruit; here it has prepared them. The vines (gefen) blossoming and spreading their fragrance completes the agricultural picture. The repeated invitation qumi... lekhi lakh (v13b = v10b) frames the natural description as an urgent summons.
14: יוֹנָתִי (yonati) — 'my dove' — the diminutive form of yonah (dove), carrying tender endearment. בְּחַגְוֵי הַסֶּלַע (bechagvei hasela) — 'in the clefts of the rock' — the rock and its clefts are places of divine protection (Exod 33:22: Moses placed in the cleft of the rock while God's glory passes; Isa 2:21: hiding in the clefts from divine judgment). The beloved hidden in the rock-cleft is protected and hidden; the lover desires to see her face and hear her voice — the very things that YHWH withholds at Sinai (Exod 33:20-23) but gives in covenant relationship.
15: שׁוּעָלִים (shu'alim) — 'foxes' — or possibly jackals; the same word covers both in biblical Hebrew. The complaint that small foxes ruin vineyards (while large predators are more visible) is a proverbial observation: small, cunning destructors often do more damage than obvious threats. The vineyard metaphor (established in 1:6) makes this a warning about what threatens the relationship: small, overlooked, cunning things that nibble at the growth. The instruction to 'catch the little foxes' is addressed to an unspecified plural — perhaps both lovers, perhaps the community.
16: דּוֹדִי לִי וַאֲנִי לוֹ (dodi li va'ani lo) — 'my beloved is mine and I am his' — the mutual-possession formula appears three times in the Song (2:16; 6:3; 7:10), each time with slightly different emphasis and word order. Here the beloved claims him first (dodi li = my beloved belongs to me); in 6:3 the order reverses (ani ledodi vedodi li = I am my beloved's and my beloved is mine); in 7:10 only her belonging to him is stated. Scholars note the progression as a deepening of her self-giving. הָרֹעֶה בַּשּׁוֹשַׁנִּים (haro'eh bashoshanim) — 'grazing among the lilies' — the beloved grazes in the lilies as a shepherd; both pastoral and intimate.
17: עַד שֶׁיָּפוּחַ הַיּוֹם (ad sheyafuach hayom) — 'until the day breathes/breaks' — pua (to breathe, to blow gently) is rare; it describes the gentle movement of the day's first breath of air. The phrase 'the day breathes' is a poetic description of dawn when the morning breeze stirs. נָסוּ הַצְּלָלִים (nasu hatzelalim) — 'and the shadows flee' — the shadows that persisted through the night give way to the first light. The invitation 'turn, my beloved' (sov demeh-lekha dodi) echoes the invitation to 'be like a gazelle or young stag' — the morning energy of the pursued/pursuing beloved closing the night-time distance.
